methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Nth Cycle, a Growth-stage climate and energy company founded by Megan O'Connor and Chad Vecitis, has raised $60M in equity alongside a $1.1B ten-year offtake agreement with Trafigura. The valuation exercise draws from a comp universe of nine Climate & Energy peers: one public company (Linamar, ticker LNZA) and eight private firms including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen.

Traditional peer-median methodology is not applicable here. The narrow peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables at a similar valuation tier—zero datapoints met the clustering criteria. The broader Climate & Energy sector population of nine peers does not provide the minimum three-point density required for a statistically defensible public or private market multiple. Attempting to anchor valuation to an all-sector median would introduce substantial comparability noise given the heterogeneity in capital intensity, technology maturity, and commercial traction across climate tech subsectors.

The analysis therefore cannot rely on conventional trading or transaction multiples. Key valuation uncertainties center on the offtake agreement's embedded optionality and credit quality, the company's path to positive unit economics at scale, and the absence of granular financial metrics (revenue, EBITDA, capex requirements) necessary to anchor discounted cash flow analysis. Any valuation work requires either expanded private peer financials or direct cash flow modeling grounded in engineering and customer contract terms.
